design_philosophy.core_loop: High-speed traversal through expansive side-scrolling levels while collecting rings as health, defeating enemies, and navigating obstacle courses with springs, slopes, loops, and hazards
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Speed vs. precision platforming—momentum-based movement creates risk of overshooting platforms or falling into pits, balanced against ring collection for damage mitigation
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Playing as Sonic, a super-fast hedgehog with a loyal sidekick (Tails), racing through colorful themed zones to collect Chaos Emeralds and stop Doctor Robotnik's evil schemes
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Sonic 2 is deliberately faster and more ambitious than its predecessor, with larger levels, doubled unique zone themes, and spin dash mechanic enabling dynamic speed control. The game prioritizes fluid high-speed platforming over precision timing, treating momentum as a core mechanic rather than an obstacle. Ring-based health system removes traditional lives pressure, shifting focus to exploration and flow state.

difficulty.lives_system: Player starts with 3 lives. Lives lost when hit without rings, falling into pits, getting crushed, drowning, or exceeding 10-minute time limit per act. Game Over on life depletion.
difficulty.checkpointing: Checkpoints placed throughout acts to save progress
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Ring collection provides damage mitigation—having at least one ring lets player survive enemy/obstacle collision; rings scatter and blink before disappearing, allowing brief recovery. Power-ups (shields, invincibility) add protection layers. Lives replenished by collecting 100 rings or 1-up items. Multiple play options: control Sonic+Tails simultaneously, or Sonic/Tails individually.

progression.upgrade_system: Spin Dash mechanic (new to Sonic 2) allows player to curl while stationary for speed boost, adding player expression to momentum management. Power-ups (shields, invincibility) provide temporary defensive buffs but are consumable.
progression.unlock_mechanism: 11 zones with 20 acts total (mostly 2-act zones vs. first game's 3-act structure). Each zone culminates in boss battle against Doctor Robotnik. Progression is linear through zones with no branching paths.
replayability_hooks: Special stages featuring pre-rendered 3D graphics (implied as separate progression track)
Choice to play as Sonic or Tails individually adds character-specific routing
Larger acts than first game encourage exploration for alternate paths

world_building_techniques: Zone naming and visual theming (11 unique themed zones: Aquatic Ruin Zone mentioned explicitly)
Environmental storytelling through level design (water hazards, technological elements in robot-themed areas)
Narrative framing: Doctor Robotnik traps animal inhabitants in aggressive robots; player must collect Chaos Emeralds to stop the Death Egg space station
Character introduction: Miles 'Tails' Prower introduced as Sonic's sidekick, present as companion throughout journey

boss_design.philosophy: Each zone concludes with a boss battle against Doctor Robotnik, establishing him as persistent antagonist across all 11 zones. Boss design serves as skill gates marking zone completion and testing mastery of zone-specific mechanics.

faction_indicators: Doctor Robotnik as central antagonist seeking Chaos Emeralds for Death Egg
Animal inhabitants of West Side Island as implicit faction needing protection from robot traps
danger_signals: Death Egg space station as existential threat powered by Chaos Emeralds
Robot enemies deployed across zones as occupation force
Bottomless pits, water hazards, spikes as environmental dangers within captured zones
environmental_storytelling: Aquatic Ruin Zone suggests water-based ecosystem corrupted by industrial hazards
Zone progression implies geographical journey across West Side Island
Robot enemy placement and zone theming reinforce industrial occupation narrative
